content.problemStatement:
Consider a weighted, undirected and connected graph with **N** nodes, numbered 1 to **N**, and **M** edges. Find the minimum cost of any walk from node **S** to node **T** , via node **V** (visiting node **V** is compulsory) , where cost of a walk is the sum of weights of distinct edges occurring in the walk. This means that even if an edge occurs multiple times in a walk, its weight is added only once to the cost of the walk.



**Warning:** Large input data, use faster methods for Input.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines followed by M edge lines. The first line of a test case contains two integers N and M. The second line contains three integers S, T and V. Then M lines follow, each containing three integers a, b and c denoting an undirected edge between a and b with weight c.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing the minimum possible cost of a walk from S to T that visits V (edges counted only once).

solution.approach: The cost of a walk is the sum of weights of distinct edges used. For three required vertices S, V and T the optimal walk can be transformed into a tree that connects the three vertices. In any undirected weighted graph the minimum weight tree connecting three terminals is a Steiner tree of size three. It is known that for three terminals the optimal Steiner tree can be rooted at some vertex X and consists of the three shortest paths X‑S, X‑V and X‑T. Therefore the answer equals min_{X in V} (dist(S,X)+dist(V,X)+dist(T,X)). We run Dijkstra from S, V and T to obtain all‑pairs shortest distances to every vertex, then scan all vertices to find the minimum sum. Each Dijkstra runs in O((N+M) log N) using a binary heap, so the total per test case is O((N+M) log N) time and O(N+M) memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O((N+M) log N) per test case
